key_insight: Personalizing a pneumatically actuated soft robotic exoglove with hand scans and FEM improves fit, joint alignment, and targeted finger mobility for dexterous rehabilitation.
engineering_relevance: The work shows how anatomy-aware design and pHRI modeling can guide safer, more effective soft assistive devices for fine motor rehabilitation and hand assistance.
method: Topological hand scans used to tailor glove geometry and silicone mold fabrication.
Finite element analysis modeled actuator bending and pHRI contact forces with a personalized finger model.
Pressure-control experiments tested static and dynamic flexion of MCP and PIP joints.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports successful anatomical fit, sufficient joint mobilization, and improved actuator-to-finger alignment when the strain-limiting layer was relaxed, supported by simulation and pneumatic testing.
watch_item: The simplified biomechanical finger model and non-ideal proximal phalanx compression suggest that full anatomical realism and comfort under repeated use still need validation.
paper_title: Soft Robotic Exogloves for Dexterous Mobility -- Towards Personalized Rehabilitation
